sentences.text: Yeah?
She was actually-- we were liberated the same camp.
No.
My name is Hela Shapow, and I was born November 5, 1925.
What else?
I was in a city, Ozorkow, Poland.
My name was Hela Storch.
S-T-O-R-C-H.
My mother's name was Bella Storch, my father's was Moshe Storch, and I had a little brother, was Shlomo Storch.
Well, my father was a very good supporter.
He made a nice living for the whole family.
He was a custom tailor.
We had a good life with my parents.
We used to go-- summer-- every summer we used to go out for three months vacation time, when we were off school.
And we lived in the country, which we loved it very much.
We had everything over there what we needed.
And when we came home from the country, fresh, relaxed, you know, children.
My mother used to be with us.
My father used to come only for the weekends.
And my childhood was a very good, and a very nice childhood way.
I had very lovely parents.
And my sister is, now-- she's alive.
My brother's alive, too.
My brother is in Los Angeles, my sister lives in Johannesburg, in South Africa.
Bluma Doman.
And what else can I tell you about my family?
My mother had a big family.
She had three-- three sisters, and she was the fourth, and she had a brother.
And each of them had three, four children.
So we had back there a very big family.
When it came a holiday, we didn't have to have French people as guests in our house, but we had only our family.
We had the sisters, the cousins, and the aunts and the uncles.
And life was very good until Hitler came into our life in 1939.
That starts being miserable.
What happened-- when they-- when they walked in-- I mean they occupied our city, the first day was fine.
The second day, it was OK.
But we told that this is going to be like this all the time.
So we always used to say, oh, after all, the Germans are not that bad, like people described them.
But later on, they showed us what they-- we know they knew how to do, and how to punish, and how to torture people.
When they came in to the city, next day, right next day, the third day, fourth day, they start accumulating people, and sending them to work.
They would used to do for them the work.
They brought us over to the camps where the soldiers were stationed.
They took us-- they brought us all there.
And we start doing for their work, what they supposed to do, we had to do-- the dirty work for them.
We cleaned-- I was a little girl at that time, about 13 years-- 12, 13 years.
I went to work.
We peeled potatoes for them, we peeled the vegetables for them.
We did everything they do-- they should do.
So we did it for them.
This was not enough.
I went three times a week to work this kind of work.
And then, I went for my sister, because I didn't want that my sister should go, because she was older than I was.
So I went for her, too.
So I was practically busy a whole week from Sunday-- from Monday until Sunday, I was working for them.
Then, they start punishing us, and didn't let us go out in the streets.
We had to walk on the road where cars are driving, where animals are walking.
We couldn't walk there-- we couldn't walk on the sidewalk, we had to walk there.
And then we had-- not all day, because we had the time was very limited.
We had only two hours a day to go to do some kind of a shopping, whatever you needed.
And that's it.
And the rest of the day, we had to sit in the house.
And waiting-- waiting for some kind of excitement should come from their site, or they should come into the house and beat us up, or whatever they wanted to do.
And this was going on like this quite a while.
Then after that, they decided to put us in ghetto.
So we came into ghetto, it was 19-- in July, 1942.
They picked a place out of the city, and there we lived until 1944.
We used to walk every day, every morning, to work out of the ghetto.
We worked at a place where we made the fair jackets for the German soldiers.
Worked very hard, you know-- the fingers, we had holes in our fingers.
Because you cannot sew something without a thimble.
They didn't give us thimbles to-- to use.
But we had to do with our fingers.
And we walked quite sometimes there.
My father had his own shop at that time.
And he employed his own people, because he had to work for the Germans.
He used to make for them suits, you know, fair coats for them, and all kind of work.
Until they didn't decide that don't need him anymore, so they decided that they going to liquidate, you know, the ghetto.
And one morning, we came from work, they made an-- it was-- I would say this was in German, appell, you know.
They concentrate us in a place in ghetto.
And everybody grabbed something with him.
I was by myself at that time, because I was working night shift.
And when I came home, my parents, were at work.
So I grabbed whatever I could.
We were always ready with suitcases in the house, that we knew that one day, they going to take-- tell us to go, and that's it.
So everybody had-- everyone of us, each member of the family had a packed suitcase.
I grabbed the suitcases, but how many could I take?
I took as many as I could.
We came out on the appell place, you know.
We were standing over there, until then they called the rest people from the factories.
They came home, because we didn't know what's going on.
We didn't know they were going to send us out from the home.
So when we came-- when my parents came, so they joined me, and I had all the suitcases with me.
Because I had money there.
We had money, we had jewelry, and everything.

And when stayed-- we stayed there on the appell place, they told us to leave all the suitcases in-- in a-- you know, in a place, a line, you know, in another-- in another spot, that we couldn't take with us anything.
Nothing whatsoever.
So in this from there, my sister-- when my sister came, so she says, what are we going to do?
We're going to be without even for the next day, nothing to change.
So she risk her life, she ran out from the line, and she grabbed the suitcase.
But the suitcase was not mine, it was not hers.
Probably was my parents'.
We couldn't wear my parents' clothes, we were children.
So that's what we took.
With this, we went, they took us to the station, to the train station, put us on the train.
And from there, as we came up on the train, a father came with-- it was a blind man.
He walked up with his son.
And when he came into the plain, the Germans didn't like him.
So they shoot him right there.
So this was the first excitement for us.
We saw-- we saw how they shoot us, shooting him.
And then we went to-- on the stair-- on the train, and they took us to Lodz.
We came into Lodz to ghetto-- to ghetto, it was 1942 when we came into Lodz.
We came in a very bad time, because that time was a curfew in Lodz.
We couldn't go out from the house-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Oh, my goodness.
No, no, no, no.
I have a tape.
Let them call.
Let call.
It was a curfew.
We didn't have no ration cuts, because everybody had ration cuts.
We came to Lodz.
We didn't have a-- we didn't have apartment where to live.
We didn't have any food.
We had some money.
My sister managed, you know, to grab some money.
And when we came there, we bought everything on the black market, because we didn't have any ration cards to go, you know, and pick up our rations.
So whatever we had, we used it up.
And then we didn't have any money anymore.
Finally they gave us apartment.
One room for five people.
And we lived in that room in winter, when it was winter, cold, freezing.
We had on one wall of the apartment was snow like outside.
Like outside, you see snow outside-- that's how we had snow on the wall.
And it was very unhealthy for us, unhealthy for my parents.
OK, but then, they start giving us, you know, our jobs.
They send us to work.
I worked with my father in the same factory.
My sister, worked in a different-- she worked in a factory where they're making straw shoes for the soldiers.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] No, let it ring, please.
Then we went to work.
Each of us worked in a different place.
And my mother didn't go to work, because we had to have somebody in the house to prepare something for us when we come home from work.
So we picked up for her some kind of work where she-- we produce for her the work.
When we came home from our job, till we went, we were sitting in the house, and sometimes I took it with me to work.
On my free time I used to do it for her.
And it was like-- and it was very good for her.
So she had the right that time, she had the right to get her piece of bread.
Otherwise, if she wouldn't make any-- any production, then she wouldn't be eligible to-- to get a piece of bread for herself.
Because we got only hundred gram bread for a day.
It was only one slice bread.
This was for a whole day.
And we-- at work, we used to get some kind of a soup.
And this was all our nourishment, what we got.
We worked there quite a while.
And then we send us over to another place, in a bigger place.
I used to work with my father there, too.
We worked quite long there, until they decided to liquidate ghetto.
The ghetto was, for us, worse than the concentration camp.
Much, much worse.
Because my father was very hungry all the time.
We used to give him our piece of bread-- what I had for myself, I give it to him.
Because he was so hungry, that one day he decided that he was going to commit suicide.
And then, when we were in Lodz, they called him from Kripo.
This was a police station where, if somebody went in there, he didn't end a life, his life was already finished.

But unfortunate, when they took him the first time there, they took his fingers, and they put in the door-- they opened the door, they put the fingers in the door, and they locked-- they closed the door.
You can imagine how he came home.
His nails were up here.
He was very sick that time.
But what we could, we did for him.
And he never recuperate from this because his nail were up already.
All his nails fall out his fingers.
But we did whatever we could.
We shared our little food, what we got for us, we shared with our parents because my father was so hungry.
And one day they came and they called him again to Kripo.
After a while, they called him again.
So in the morning before we got up, all of us, we didn't know what happened.
He got dressed and he throw himself down the stairs.
Because he said he was not able to go again to Kripo and to be there for so long, so many days, and to go through the torture what he went through.
And this we had to thank a woman from our city.
She came to the police, to the Kripo, and she told them that we left the house and we have a lot of valuable things left in the house.
And a lot of money and a lot of gold.
So they took him back to the city.
They gave him a sack of hundred kilo flour to took on his back.
And to run with this bag.
He was not able to do it.
He was not that strong.
After being already in Kripo for so long.
And didn't have practically nothing to eat there.
So he couldn't do that.
So they beat him so up that he came half dead person came home.
They took him again to Kripo, and he was there for another 10 days without a cover, without clothes, without a blanket.
So my sister, she was the oldest, so she used to run there and staying outside the front of the door and begging them they should let her in.
Or she'd take from her the blanket and give it to my father.
That time we though he's finished.
But he came home.
Took him quite sometimes, quite a few months until he recuperate from there.
And no food practically in ghetto.
We didn't have any food.
But the torture and the trouble what we went through, it was unbelievable.
It was not for a human being to go through something like that in life.
But, you say sometimes that to punish-- you know, to torture people who are strong.
And somehow, we survived the torture.
And one day, when they start liquidating Lodz ghetto-- so they came into my-- to our house with a picture of myself, and a picture of my father.

That we should get ready, they taking us out from the house, and away from work, and they're going to send us to another place where we're going to have work, we're going to eat, and it's going to be good for us and everything.
So we were so naive, we believe them, that this is going to happen.
But unfortunate, didn't happen.
They took-- so they accumulate probably about few hundred people in the police station, and then, from the police station, they send us-- they took us, again, on the trains.
And we didn't know where we going.
That time, we didn't know where we going.
So-- and the trains were not like passenger trains.
But it was trains, you know, where they were-- you know, transport-- transport train, you know.
With no windows, only a door.
They put us in like bunch of animals.
They closed the doors, they sealed the doors.
And then we going back and forth, back and forth for a few-- for a few-- maybe a week, to Auschwitz.
We didn't know it was Auschwitz.
We were like the sheeps there, going, everywhere they just told us to go.
We came in, in a place.
We walked down, you know, with the train.
We came-- we come there, and we see that a lot of machines, sewing machines are on the-- are on the lot.
So we were start-- you know, laughing.
Oh, we're going to work here like they told us.
So, but unfortunate, it was not like they told us.
Right away, when I came to Auschwitz, then it was, this is Auschwitz.
Then when I came with my father, I went left, and my father went right.
And since then, I didn't see him anymore.
This was the camp Auschwitz.
I came to Auschwitz.
They brought us over to the-- to the bungalows.
Where each bungalow took in about 1,000 people-- they were only woman.
And we-- we slept on those bunkers.
And I was under-- lucky, I was on the lower bunker. 10 girls slept with me on the lower bunker.
Each of us, we were laying on the side, like the-- we were like the herring, to each, next to each other.
And the top was the same thing. 1,000 people they put in, in this bungalow.
It was hot.
And we couldn't even pull out our head from under the bungalow-- I mean the bunker, to catch a little bit of air, you know.
But the Germans used to come in the morning.
If somebody had his hair-- sticked out his head, out of the bunker, so you got, with a stick, they gave us a over the head with a stick.
So we were afraid.
So everybody was inside, was quiet.
We were afraid to open up our mouths.
And 5 o'clock in the morning, they called us outside-- winter, when snow was outside.
And to punish us, we had to kneel in the snow from 5 o'clock in the morning until 8 o'clock in the morning, until they made appell for us.
Then they were-- they were counting us, if nobody run away.
They had to have the right figures, you know, of the people.
You can imagine that nobody from us could stand up anymore, because our-- we didn't have any feet anymore.
No knees, no feet, no nothing.
We came into the-- into the bungalow.
They give us something to eat.
The food was only a slice of bread.
And all day long, nothing else.
But then decided afternoon, they gave us a half an hour free to go out and to catch some air.
I walked out from the camp, and on the other side of the camp was another camp.
But this fence was electrical fence, you know.
And you couldn't get close to the fence, because you could be-- you know-- electrocyzed, right?
Electrocute.
So, I noticed on the other side from the camp, I recognize a friend of mine.
This was friend-- my girlfriend's brother.
He was before me there, already in camp.
And he note-- he recognized me, he noticed that I was walking around there.
Because they didn't shave my head that time, they just cut it short.
Everybody was shaven.
I was the only one, the lucky one, I had short hair.
So he says, what are you doing here?
I said, I just came a few days ago to this beautiful place here.
Are you hungry?
I says, what are you asking questions?
He says, stay here for a minute, I'll bring you something, and I'll throw over the fence.
So he brought me-- brought out a bag, he put in a piece of bread-- a slice of bread and an onion.
That's what he had, because he worked there as a carpenter.
So he could manage better that we did.
So he got us a little bit more food.
And he looked better than we did.
And I got that piece of-- slice of bread, with the onion, and I ran into the-- to the bunker, you know, to the bungalow.
And I sat on the bunker.
And I called the girls, the ones slept with me on the bunker.
And I says, here, I have a piece of bread.
Everybody grab a-- a little-- just a little piece, you know, just to feel like that-- that you have something in your mouth.
And this was going on-- that's it.
I didn't see him anymore, because they probably-- maybe they caught him that time, or they told him not to do it, because otherwise they're going to kill him.
And this was-- unfortunate, we were not long-- this was our luck, that we were not long in Auschwitz in camp.
Only two months.
They tortured us for two months like that-- every single morning, kneel on snow.
And then we were standing already a few times in line to get our numbers, the tattoos.
So they said, no.
They were too busy, they had too many people coming from Lodz that time, because they were liquidating the whole ghetto.
There were a lot of people coming there.
So they didn't do the-- the tattoos for us.
And they decided to send us from there to Bergen-Belsen.
In Bergen-Belsen, it was bad, but not as bad as in Auschwitz.
We came into a bunk-- into a bungalow, where we were sleeping on the straw, straw.
Only straw-- we didn't have any mattresses, nothing to cover up.
We had to sleep in clothes.
And this was a bungalow left from soldiers was-- were there.
The German soldiers were there.
The straw had a lot of fleas.
We couldn't sleep at night.
And on the wall from that bungalow was in the thousands of thousands names on the wall are written.
You were standing and looking at the wall, it was one name after the other.
Thousands of names were there.
But we thought, maybe we'd find somebody from our people.
No.
This was a camp where the soldiers, the German soldiers were there.
Maybe Russian soldiers?
Who knows who was there?
We couldn't sleep at night, because the fleas were-- we were bitten from the fleas terribly.
And we had to sleep in our clothes.
We didn't have any wash rooms to go, you know, to wash ourselves.
We had only one wash room.
You had to stay and wait until the next party will be, you know, walk out, and I could come in.
But anyway, no matter what happened, we didn't have any warm water.
We had cold, ice cold water.
But we took off all our clothes-- we were only in a camp with women.
So we took off the clothes and washed ourselves with ice cold water.
It was probably 40 degree below, you know.
It was very, very cold that time, because there was mostly wintertime.
And that's what we-- also, we were not there long.
Because they decided, why should they keep us in a camp where we don't do nothing there?
Just walking around and do nothing?
And tortured us, and no food.
So they sent to a camp where we're going to work.
They-- we lined up a line, and we went to the train again.
We were always like a-- always on the road.
Always on the go, from one camp to the other.
We came to a camp.
It was the camp-- it was a labor camp.
There was a big camp there.
And they decided to send us there.
And we came over there.
Salzwedel.
The camp was Salzwedel.
It was a very big camp.
The whole transport came there.
The whole-- the whole-- the whole transport, what we came from Auschwitz.
The same girls, the same people, came to Salzwedel.
And we came 1,000 people, 1,000 girls.
It was girls, and some-- you know, it were the mothers there, but without the children.
It was sisters, to two sisters.
I know only one was-- she had a sister.
It was one of my friend from my hometown.
She came with her sister.
But the rest of the people were all single people.
No.
